content: Quarantine and isolation are distinct public health measures. Quarantine separates and restricts movement of individuals exposed to a contagious disease during the incubation period to see if they become sick. Isolation separates infected individuals from those not infected, regardless of symptom status. Quarantine applies to asymptomatic contacts; isolation applies to confirmed cases. Duration is based on disease-specific incubation periods (quarantine) or infectious periods (isolation). Legal authority for quarantine/isolation varies by jurisdiction but generally requires public health emergency declarations.
source: CDC quarantine/isolation guidelines, WHO International Health Regulations

content: Quarantine implementation: determine exposure date, calculate duration based on maximum incubation period (e.g., 14 days for Ebola, 10 days for SARS-CoV-2), provide daily symptom monitoring, arrange testing if symptomatic, ensure basic needs (food, medical care, mental health support). Isolation implementation: separate case in dedicated room with ventilation, use PPE for caregivers, determine release criteria (time-based or test-based), monitor for complications. Home quarantine/isolation requires clear instructions, regular check-ins, and contingency plans for worsening symptoms. Facility-based quarantine needed when home conditions inadequate or high-risk exposures.
source: CDC isolation/quarantine guidance, WHO infection prevention protocols

content: Quarantine effectiveness depends on: timing (started before symptom onset), adherence (monitored through visits or technology), and support services (addressing barriers to compliance). Ethical considerations include: least restrictive means, proportionality to risk, transparency, and addressing socioeconomic impacts. Release from quarantine: symptom-free through incubation period, or negative test if test-based strategy used. Release from isolation: time-based (e.g., 5-10 days from symptom onset plus 24 hours fever-free) or test-based (two negative tests). Immunocompromised individuals may require longer isolation due to prolonged viral shedding.
source: CDC MMWR, WHO ethical considerations for public health measures
